planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Purple de Milpa is a traditional small-fruited tomatillo of the Mexican milpa system, highly prized by Mexican cooks for its richer, more complex flavor and purple-green to deep purple color compared to the standard large-fruited green tomatillos used in commercial salsa verde production.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost and provide staking or caging as plants become sprawling. Purple de Milpa is a traditional Mexican tomatillo variety grown in the traditional milpa polyculture system; it produces small (1–1.5 inch diameter), round fruits with papery husks that burst and split when ripe, ripening to purple-green to deep purple-green rather than the standard yellow-green of commercial tomatillos. The flavor is more concentrated, fruitier, and tangier than standard green tomatillos, with a more complex background of citrus, herbs, and earthiness that many Mexican cooks and chefs consider superior for salsa verde, enchiladas verdes, and mole verde when an authentic deep flavor is desired. Plant in pairs or groups as tomatillos need cross-pollination for best fruit set; highly productive in warm weather.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; the standard green heirloom tomatillo produces medium to large (1.5–2 inch) green to yellow-green fruits in papery husks and is the backbone of Mexican salsa verde, enchiladas verdes, and countless other dishes — plant at least two plants for cross-pollination.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost into fertile, well-drained soil; stake or cage plants as they sprawl. Green tomatillo is the standard culinary tomatillo of Mexican cuisine — not a green tomato but a distinct species (Physalis ixocarpa) with a characteristic papery husk that expands around the developing fruit; fruits are harvested when the husk splits and the fruit fills it completely, at a bright green to yellow-green stage with a tangy, tart, citrus-herbal flavor that is distinctly different from any tomato flavor. The tart flavor of the fresh tomatillo is the foundation of salsa verde, a primary sauce in Mexican cooking alongside salsa roja; tomatillos are also used in mole verde, enchiladas verdes, chile verde, and canned as salsa. Highly productive and heat-tolerant; does extremely well in Gulf Coast and Southwestern gardens. Two or more plants are needed for pollination — a single plant produces little to no fruit.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Pineapple ground cherry is a named selection of the cape gooseberry type with especially sweet, intensely tropical pineapple-vanilla flavor in small, papery-husked fruits that drop from the plant when ripe — an extraordinary, underused fruit for snacking, baking, and preserves.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost into well-drained, fertile soil in full sun. Pineapple Ground Cherry is a named selection of the ground cherry (Physalis pruinosa) grown for its small (1/2–3/4 inch), golden-yellow fruits enclosed in dry, papery, tan husks (lantern-like husks much smaller than tomatillo husks) with an intensely sweet, tropical flavor reminiscent of pineapple, vanilla, and caramel — completely unlike tomatillos in flavor despite being related. The fruits drop from the plant when fully ripe and are gathered from the ground (hence the name); this drop-when-ripe characteristic is the easiest ripeness indicator. Extraordinarily productive in warm weather, with hundreds of fruits per plant. Delicious eaten fresh, in pies (often compared to cape gooseberry pies), in jams, and dried like raisins. A charming and unusual fruit for Gulf Coast and Southern gardens that is barely known to most gardeners.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Cossack Pineapple is a well-known named variety of ground cherry with especially large fruits (for a ground cherry) and an intense pineapple-vanilla flavor, reportedly brought from Russia to the US and long maintained in the Seed Savers Exchange network as one of the finest ground cherry selections.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. Cossack Pineapple is a well-regarded heirloom ground cherry variety that produces larger fruits than most ground cherry types — up to 3/4 inch — with an intense, complex flavor combining pineapple, vanilla, and a citrus-caramel sweetness that makes it one of the most acclaimed varieties in Seed Savers Exchange evaluations. Like other ground cherries, the fruits are enclosed in papery husks (calyces) that dry and tan as the fruit ripens, and the fruit drops naturally when ripe; collect from the ground and remove the papery husk before eating. Delicious fresh, in pies (ground cherry pie has been popular in the Midwest and Appalachia for generations), in preserves, and dried. Self-sows readily and can naturalize in the garden if left to drop seed.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; Suyo Long is a traditional Chinese burpless cucumber with exceptionally long (12–18 inch), ribbed, slightly curved fruits, tender thin skin, seedless-to-few-seed interior, and mild, sweet, non-bitter flavor — one of the finest cucumbers for fresh eating and extraordinarily productive in hot weather.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost when soil is above 65°F; train vines up a trellis to keep the long fruits straight and prevent rot — trellising is particularly important for Suyo Long to develop straight fruits. Suyo Long is a traditional Chinese slicing cucumber producing very long (12–18 inch), slightly ribbed, tapered fruits with a distinctive light green, slightly warted skin and an interior that is almost entirely flesh with very few seeds; the flavor is exceptionally mild, sweet, and refreshing with none of the bitterness common in cucumbers grown in heat stress. It is the burpless cucumber par excellence — the thin skin and low cucurbitacin content make it highly digestible and virtually non-gassy even when eaten in large quantities. Extremely productive in hot weather and much more heat-tolerant than European greenhouse cucumbers; outstanding for Gulf Coast and Southern summer production. Best trellised vertically for straight fruits and improved air circulation.
sources: Kitazawa Seed Company Asian Vegetable Guide
